issue: Transparency and accountability of nuclear arsenals and doctrines

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights its recent disclosures of stockpile numbers and transparency on nuclear doctrine, budgets, and modernization, noting similar efforts by France and the UK. It urges Russia and China to adopt equivalent levels of transparency and accountability.
entries.quote: For example, the United States has published the number of weapons in our nuclear stockpile twice in the past four years, including most recently last year. We have also been transparent with respect to nuclear doctrine, military budgets, and nuclear modernization. France and the UK have also demonstrated a commitment to transparency, including through the presentation of their national reports during side events during this PrepCom. We call on Russia and China to commit to the same level of transparency and accountability that we have demonstrated.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency by annually publishing the Stockpile Stewardship and Management Plan along with detailed budget information for Congress and the public. This signals openness about nuclear stockpile management and associated funding.
entries.quote: Separately, we release a detailed report every year, titled the Stockpile Stewardship and Management Plan. Both this document and equally detailed budget information are made available to the U.S. Congress and the public each year.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes its consistent national reporting on NPT implementation and notes the growth in reporting by States parties. It supports dedicating formal time at PrepCom and Review Conferences for presenting and discussing national reports to enhance transparency and accountability.
entries.quote: 8. The United States has reported consistently on its own implementation of the Non-Proliferation Treaty and actions to strengthen the Treaty regime across all three pillars, including to the 2014 Preparatory Conference, the 2015 Review Conference and, most recently, the 2022 Review Conference (NPT/CONF.2020/57), using a reporting format adopted by the nuclear-weapon States. The practice of submitting national reports has grown steadily and is now well established. In 2015, 22 States parties submitted national reports, and in 2022, 40 States parties did so. These reports have usually been presented in side events, as there has not been place for such presentations at formal meetings of the Preparatory Committee or the Review Conference. The United States would support dedicating time at those formal meetings for presentation and interactive discussion of national reports. Such discussions would provide an enhanced mechanism for transparency and accountability.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports CTBT entry into force, backs its verification regime, funds and supplies IMS data, and continues its zero‑yield testing moratorium while urging China and Russia to adhere to similar moratoria and the DPRK to declare and maintain one, with greater transparency from all. It hosts observers at the Nevada National Security Site and commits to transparency in stockpile stewardship. The United States also supports negotiating an FMCT at the Conference on Disarmament in line with CD/1299 as a key next step toward disarmament.
entries.quote: 2000 Steps One and Two; 2010 Actions Ten through Fourteen: CTBT and Test Moratorium: On Steps One and Two, the United States remains committed to the CTBT’s entry into force and strongly supports the treaty’s verification regime. The United States is the largest financial contributor to the CTBTO PrepCom, provides significant voluntary contributions, and maintains high data availability from the IMS stations the United States is responsible for. The United States has not conducted a nuclear explosive test since 1992 and continues to observe its zero- yield moratorium on nuclear explosive testing. We urge the PRC and Russia to adhere to their respective zero-yield moratoria on nuclear explosive testing and the DPRK to declare and maintain such moratorium. We also urge the PRC, Russia, and the DPRK to provide transparency into their testing programs. For its part, the United States has continued hosting international observers at the Nevada National Security Site (NNSS), and we are committed to transparency in our stockpile stewardship and nonproliferation activities at NNSS and other nuclear security enterprise sites. 2000 Steps Three and Four; 2010 Actions Six and Fifteen: Progress on FMCT and Other Efforts at the CD: On Steps Three and Four, we support negotiation of an FMCT at the Conference on Disarmament as the essential next multilateral step toward achieving the ultimate goal of a world without nuclear weapons. Negotiations, which should take place in accordance with CD/1299, are long overdue.

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights it has declassified information on its nuclear stockpile and argues that greater transparency of nuclear arsenals supports nonproliferation and disarmament, including fulfilling NPT commitments. It frames transparency as a concrete step toward meeting treaty obligations.
entries.quote: And it is why the United States just released newly declassified information on our nuclear weapons stockpile. We believe that increasing the transparency of states’ nuclear stockpiles is important to nonproliferation and disarmament efforts, including commitments under this treaty.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports dedicating time for interactive discussion of national reports at future NPT meetings and advocates reporting by both nuclear-weapon and non-nuclear-weapon states to ensure balanced attention to all three pillars.
entries.quote: I wish to reaffirm that the United States remains ready and willing to allocate time for the interactive discussion of national reports in future NPT meetings. Further, to ensure a balanced treatment across all three pillars of the Treaty, we continue to believe there is value in including reporting by both nuclear weapons states and non-nuclear weapons states.

entries.position_summary: The United States underscores its commitment to transparency by voluntarily releasing aggregate New START data in May 2023. It criticizes Russia for not providing comparable data under the treaty.
entries.quote: Moreover, the United States voluntarily released aggregate data on U.S. strategic offensive arms under the New START Treaty on May 15, 2023. We note that Russia did not.

entries.position_summary: Supports regular reporting by States parties on implementing the 2010 Action Plan and Article VI, and encourages nuclear-weapon States to adopt standardized reporting formats and agreed intervals within the strengthened review process.
entries.quote: 7. States parties have long reported to the Review Conference on their actions in support of the Non-Proliferation Treaty. As was stated in the consensus action plan from the 2010 Review Conference, “States parties should submit regular reports, within the framework of the strengthened review process for the Treaty, on the implementation of the present action plan, as well as of article VI”, along with disarmament actions recommended by the 1995 and 2000 Review Conferences (action 20). The action plan also encouraged the nuclear-weapon States to agree on a standard reporting form and to determine appropriate reporting intervals (action 21).

entries.position_summary: The United States supports enhancing transparency and accountability by dedicating plenary time during PrepCom and Review Conference meetings to consider national reports, particularly those of nuclear-weapon states. It endorses a simplified alternative proposal that nearly achieved consensus after the Chair’s paper failed to do so.
entries.quote: 10. The working group held extensive and constructive deliberations on proposals for transparency and accountability, focused on interactive discussion of national reports by States parties. Paragraphs 16 to 19 of the Chair’s working paper address this issue in detail, but fall short of consensus. A simplified alternative proposal submitted by four States parties came very close to achieving consensus, with only one State party objecting. The United States believes that this alternative, reproduced below, represents the best chance of gaining consensus in this area: The working group recommends that the Chairs of the Preparatory Committee meetings and the President of the Review Conference dedicate time in plenary sessions of the review cycle to consider the national reports of States parties, in particular the reports of the nuclear-weapon States, with regard to the 24-09063 3/5

entries.position_summary: The United States supports negotiating an FMCT, has engaged in UNGA FMCT initiatives, and maintains a unilateral moratorium on producing fissile material for weapons while urging China to follow suit. It cites irreversible disarmament steps—dismantling plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions via arms control with Russia, and fissile material disposition—and endorses the principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. The U.S. advances verification capabilities through the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE.
entries.quote: We will also engage constructively in the other subsidiary bodies, including on the Cessation of the nuclear arms race and nuclear disarmament. In addition to our efforts at the CD, the United States actively participated in two UN General Assembly FMCT initiatives in 2014-2015 and 2017-2018 that we believe laid important groundwork for future FMCT negotiations. We continue to maintain our unilateral moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons and urge the PRC – the only Nuclear Weapon State that has not done the same – to join us. 2000 Steps Five and Thirteen; 2010 Actions Two and Eighteen: Irreversibility and Further Development of Verification Capabilities: On Steps Five and Thirteen, the United States has undertaken important steps to support the principal of irreversibility, including through irreversible dismantlement of all weapons-grade plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions through arms control treaties with Russia, disposition of fissile material from nuclear weapons, and other efforts. We also support language from the 2022 RevCon Draft Final document that acknowledges the mutually reinforcing principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. Active U.S. engagement in the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE underscore our commitment to engaging a broad set of countries on the development of future tools for arms control that support irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency in nuclear disarmament. We also welcome the group statement on irreversibility in nuclear disarmament delivered by Norway,

entries.position_summary: The United States supports national reporting and greater transparency under the NPT, noting its own release of updated nuclear stockpile data. It regrets that one state blocked the national reports proposal and advocates incorporating transparency-focused discussions into formal RevCon meetings to improve deliberations.
entries.quote: The United States deeply regrets that one state blocked the proposal on national reports. We have long been committed to transparency in our NPT commitments, exemplified most recently by our release of updated information on our nuclear weapons stockpile. We have previously held side-events to describe our NPT commitments and engage in discussion with RevCon participants, and we think bringing that type of discussion into our formal meetings would improve the quality and focus of our deliberations.

entries.position_summary: The country commits to placing all civilian nuclear facilities under IAEA safeguards and annually reports its civil plutonium holdings, with plans to also report civil HEU holdings. This reflects support for robust safeguards and greater transparency to prevent diversion of civilian fissile material to weapons.
entries.quote: To help provide confidence that fissile material in civil use is not diverted to weapons, we make all our civilian nuclear facilities available for IAEA safeguards and we report annually to the IAEA our civil plutonium holdings; we are also looking at taking steps to report our civil holdings of highly-enriched uranium.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts that it is not the United States but another state that is undertaking a large peacetime nuclear buildup. It criticizes the minimal transparency surrounding that buildup, implying concern over unchecked arsenal expansion and opacity.
entries.quote: It is also not the United States that is actively engaging in one of the largest peacetime nuclear build-ups in history, with minimal transparency into those activities.

entries.position_summary: The country asserts that China is rapidly expanding its nuclear weapons capabilities and doing so opaquely. This reflects concern over both the growth of China’s arsenal and its lack of transparency.
entries.quote: The People’s Republic of China (PRC) continues a rapid and opaque expansion of its nuclear weapons capabilities.

entries.position_summary: The United States is proactively convening expert discussions among the five nuclear-weapon States focused on nuclear doctrines and risk reduction. It seeks to enhance transparency and build confidence despite acknowledged difficulties.
entries.quote: That is why the United States has convened multiple expert discussions on nuclear doctrines and risk reduction among the five nuclear-weapon States – despite the obvious difficulties to doing so.

entries.position_summary: The United States underscores regular national reporting to NPT meetings, including its Nuclear Posture Review, as evidence of a strong commitment to transparency and progress on disarmament. It supports enhancing reporting and interactive discussion to improve transparency and accountability in the NPT review process and will incorporate state feedback to expand future reports.
entries.quote: The United States submitted national reports to the 2014 NPT Preparatory Committee, an update for the 2015 NPT Review Conference, an update including on actions over the previous six years for the 2022 Review Conference, and an update to that report providing a link to our 2022 Nuclear Posture Review. Before the practice of “national reports,” the United States submitted similar information to previous Review Conferences. Collectively, these demonstrate the steady U.S. commitment to transparency and making real progress on disarmament. We welcome constructive efforts to improve NPT reporting and strongly support calls to improve transparency and accountability in the NPT review process through interactive discussion of national reports. As we approach future U.S. reports, we will consider feedback from interested States regarding specific transparency elements they would find helpful. Based on that feedback, we will seek to address additional topics in our future national reports where possible.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency as a priority, using it to support arms control efforts and to build confidence among NPT parties. It views openness as a means to strengthen mutual trust and the effectiveness of arms control.
entries.quote: Finally, the United States prioritizes transparency, whether as a tool of arms control or to build confidence among NPT parties.

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights transparency by publishing historical production data for plutonium and highly enriched uranium for weapons. It has declared hundreds of tons of this material excess and subjected their storage and disposition to IAEA verification.
entries.quote: With respect to existing fissile material, we have made public historical U.S. production of plutonium and highly enriched uranium for nuclear weapons, and we have declared hundreds of tons of such material excess, in many cases with IAEA verification of its storage and disposition.

entries.position_summary: The country pledges negative security assurances to all NPT non-nuclear-weapon states in compliance with their obligations. It also reaffirms that none of its nuclear weapons are targeted at any state, a de-targeting measure that serves as a confidence-building step. These statements, made via its Nuclear Posture Review, reflect transparency about its nuclear policy.
entries.quote: As stated in our Nuclear Posture Review, we also provide negative security assurances to all NPT non-nuclear-weapon states that are in compliance with their nuclear nonproliferation obligations. Further, we have repeatedly reaffirmed that none of our nuclear weapons are targeted at any other state.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports robust transparency measures, including those within the NPT review process, to reduce misunderstandings and build confidence. It emphasizes aligning words and actions and adhering to prior NPT review conference consensus decisions.
entries.quote: I also wish to reemphasize that it is the United States’ view that transparency measures, including those taken through this review process, can play a critical role in reducing misunderstandings and reassuring others that a state’s words and actions are in alignment, including as it pertains to prior consensus decisions of NPT review conferences.

entries.position_summary: The country supports preserving and expanding attention to cross-cutting issues—transparency/reporting, education and capacity-building, and gender/diversity—within the NPT. It proposes dedicating plenary sessions at Review Conferences and the Preparatory Committee to these topics.
entries.quote: • Preserving and potentially expanding opportunities to address cross-cutting issues, such as transparency, reporting, education and capacity-building, gender and diversity. These could be addressed in dedicated plenary sessions of review conferences and the Preparatory Committee.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes high transparency standards for its nuclear policies and supports incorporating structured dialogue, such as national reports, into the NPT review process, regretting that this was blocked. It calls for the urgent negotiation of a Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty to prevent a renewed nuclear arms race and lay groundwork for further progress.
entries.quote: It is why the United States prioritizes high standards for transparency of our nuclear policies and programs. We welcome discussion on our national report, and deeply regret that certain parties prevented the Working Group from adopting such structured dialogue a regular part of the NPT review process.] It is also why we believe the time has come for a long overdue – and urgently needed – Fissile Material Cutoff Treaty. Without it, we risk a return to an era of nuclear arms racing. With it, we can set a better foundation for progress on

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China’s rapid, opaque nuclear buildup and rejects China’s proposed No First Use treaty as non-credible and unverifiable. It also condemns Russia’s coercive nuclear rhetoric and highlights concern over Russia’s increased military cooperation with the DPRK.
entries.quote: Such falsehoods distract from the truly worrisome issues at hand, including China’s rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up, and Russia’s coercive nuclear rhetoric and increased military cooperation with the DPRK. We were dismayed by some States Parties’ support for China’s proposal for a non- credible, unverifiable No First Use Treaty. We must ask – what good is a statement from China that it will not use nuclear weapons first, when its nuclear build-up appears to contradict to its stated policy?

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency through its NPR and stockpile disclosures, reports steep unilateral reductions including in non-strategic weapons, and highlights measures lowering operational status and the role of nuclear weapons in its strategy. It encourages China and Russia to adopt similar transparency. This is presented as part of fulfilling disarmament commitments and advancing risk reduction.
entries.quote: well as in the public release of information in our Nuclear Posture Review and pertaining to our nuclear weapons stockpile. The U.S. national report to the 2022 RevCon also provided detailed information on other relevant topics, including steep U.S. reductions in non-strategic nuclear weapons and other unilateral cuts beyond treaty-based reductions over the past three decades, significant measures taken to reduce operational status of nuclear weapons systems, and the reduced role of nuclear weapons in U.S. national security strategy, including with respect to the 2022 NPR’s Declaratory Policy. We encourage the PRC and Russia to provide similar transparency.

entries.position_summary: The United States criticizes China’s rapid, opaque expansion of its nuclear arsenal, citing new ICBM silos, delivery systems, and ongoing production of fissile material, with projections of 1,000+ warheads by 2030. It argues China’s buildup contradicts its stated policies of minimum deterrence and No First Use and contrasts with other NPT nuclear-weapon states’ behavior. The U.S. asserts it acts responsibly as a nuclear-weapon state under the NPT.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, the United States is committed to responsible behavior as a nuclear-weapon State under the NPT. Not all NPT nuclear-weapon States have done the same. We continue to be deeply concerned by China’s rapid, opaque, and destabilizing expansion of its nuclear arsenal, including the construction of hundreds of new ICBM silos and the development of new delivery systems. We assess that China currently has 600 operational nuclear weapons, which will increase to over 1,000 by the year 2030, and that it is actively producing new fissile material for use in its growing nuclear arsenal, unlike the other four nuclear-weapon states. China’s activities indicate it seeks the ability to inflict far greater levels of overwhelming damage to an adversary in a nuclear exchange than it has historically deemed necessary, and all while professing to apply “minimum deterrence” and “No First Use” in its nuclear authorities.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China’s rapid and opaque nuclear build-up, questioning the credibility and clarity of its no-first-use policy and broader doctrine. It notes Beijing’s reluctance to engage substantively and expresses skepticism about a proposed no-first-use treaty, particularly regarding how it would be verified. Overall, it views China’s initiative as lacking substance.
entries.quote: The PRC’s actions, in particular its rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up, raise questions about Beijing’s already ambiguous, stated “no first use” policy and its nuclear doctrine more broadly, calling into question what such an initiative aims to achieve. The PRC, to date, has resisted substantive engagement to answer these questions. We also continue to have concerns about how its proposed no first use treaty would operate in practice, including with respect to verification. We see more style than substance.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes China for rapidly and opaquely expanding its nuclear arsenal and warns it could surpass 1,000 warheads by 2030, posing a threat to global peace and stability.
entries.quote: As we speak, China is rapidly and opaquely building up its nuclear arsenal, threatening global peace and stability. We assess that Beijing will exceed 1,000 nuclear warheads by the year 2030.

entries.position_summary: The country regrets the lack of consensus on measures to enhance transparency and accountability and supports structured discussion of national reports. It favors strengthening reporting and dialogue mechanisms within the NPT review process.
entries.quote: I share the disappointment expressed by many that we did not achieve consensus on concrete proposals. In particular, on measures related to improving transparency and accountability, such as through structured discussion of national reports.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes China for expanding its nuclear arsenal while withholding basic information on stockpiles, fissile materials, and nuclear posture. It argues China’s lack of transparency and refusal to join arms control efforts undermines its commitment to NPT objectives and the broader nonproliferation regime.
entries.quote: Meanwhile, China refuses to disclose even the most basic information about its growing stockpile size, fissile material holdings and production, or to answer basic questions regarding its nuclear posture. Its actions, lack of transparency, and especially its refusal to join arms control efforts raise serious questions about its commitment to the NPT’s objectives, undermining the global nonproliferation regime.

entries.position_summary: The country reports an 88% reduction of its nuclear stockpile and seeks to further lessen the role of nuclear weapons in its defense strategy, while acknowledging current security conditions. This frames its actions as contributing to disarmament and provides a degree of transparency.
entries.quote: We have reduced the number of nuclear weapons in our stockpile by over 88% and, while fully cognizant of the current security environment, continue to seek opportunities to reduce the role of nuclear weapons in our national defense strategy.

entries.position_summary: Supports greater transparency by nuclear-weapon states; the United States exemplifies this by publishing the size of its nuclear stockpile.
entries.quote: Some nuclear weapon states have shown significant transparency. The United States published the number of weapons in our nuclear stockpile 3

entries.position_summary: The United States supports hosting regional and topical consultations by NPT review leadership and is open to dedicating time for presentation and interactive discussion of national reports, provided all other nuclear-weapon states do the same. This indicates conditional support for enhanced transparency and a more structured, interactive review process.
entries.quote: • hosting of regional and topical consultations by the RevCon President-designate and the PrepCom Chairs As indicated earlier, the United States can support other good ideas, such as dedicating time to the presentation and interactive discussion of national reports, so long as all other nuclear weapon States do the same.

entries.position_summary: The country urges creation of a new transparency and accountability mechanism, open to all nuclear-weapon states and other participants, based on a near-consensus proposal from last year, to be implemented next year. They want this mechanism to inform and enable the 2026 Review Conference to adopt improvements for future review cycles.
entries.quote: Chair, we recommend you and your successor work towards a new mechanism for transparency and accountability, to be implemented as soon as next year. We believe it should involve all the nuclear weapons states and others that may choose to participate and should be based on the proposal that nearly gained consensus last year. This could provide the basis for the Review Conference in 2026 to decide on improvements for future review cycles.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes China for expanding its nuclear forces in ways seen as inconsistent with its stated No First Use policy and traditional minimum deterrent posture. It urges China to increase transparency and engage in arms control by answering outstanding questions at the PrepCom.
entries.quote: stated “No First Use” policy and strays from its historic focus on a “minimum deterrent” force structure. China, to date, has resisted calls to explain its build-up or engage on arms control. During this PrepCom, we hope China will abandon its approach of distractions and avoidance and instead demonstrate responsibility by answering the questions many of us have posed over the last two PrepComs.

entries.position_summary: The country calls on the P5 to adopt concrete risk-reduction steps, including crisis communication channels, formalized ballistic missile launch notifications, and a commitment to maintain a human-in-the-loop for nuclear command and employment. It regrets that two of the five nuclear-weapon states have not engaged substantively with these proposals.
entries.quote: We proposed risk reduction steps to the P5, such as establishing crisis communication channels among the five nuclear-weapon states, formalizing ballistic missile launch notifications, and committing to maintain a “human-in-the- loop” for the command, control, and employment of nuclear weapons. We regret that two of these states have yet to engage substantively on this agenda.

entries.position_summary: The country presses Russia to uphold arms control and pursue risk reduction, rejecting Russia’s purported suspension of New START as legally invalid and condemning its nuclear rhetoric for heightening risks of miscalculation. It also criticizes China’s rapid and opaque nuclear buildup, calling into question Beijing’s transparency and intentions.
entries.quote: We continue to press Russia on arms control and risk reduction despite its legally invalid purported suspension of the New START Treaty and its rhetoric regarding nuclear weapons that not only increase nuclear risks but also heighten the possibility of miscalculation. Meanwhile, China’s rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up raises questions about Beijing’s 2

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights transparency by publishing detailed data on its nuclear stockpile and fissile materials, including materials removed from weapons use, historical production, and civil plutonium reporting. This supports confidence and provides a basis for verification of disarmament-related activities.
entries.quote: Detailed information on the U.S. nuclear weapons stockpile is also included in our national report, as is information on fissile materials removed from nuclear weapons use, historical production of such materials, and reporting on civil plutonium.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes addressing the threat from Russian and Chinese nuclear arsenals while showcasing its own transparency by publicly releasing stockpile numbers. It criticizes China and Russia for opaque build-ups and failing to reciprocate on transparency.
entries.quote: He has made clear his desire to address the threat posed by Russia and China’s nuclear arsenals. In sharp contrast to China’s opaque nuclear weapons build-up, the United States has been remarkably transparent, releasing the number of weapons in our stockpile twice in the last four years. However, for too long U.S. leadership and transparency have not been met with reciprocity by China and Russia.

entries.position_summary: The United States (with France and the UK) emphasizes transparency and responsible practices, declaring excess fissile material and placing it and all civilian nuclear facilities under IAEA safeguards while annually reporting civil plutonium holdings. It frames these steps as risk-reduction measures that prevent miscalculation and support progress on disarmament commitments under the NPT. The statement contrasts this approach with what it describes as the opacity of China and Russia.
entries.quote: On Steps Eight and Ten, the United States has also declared hundreds of tons of fissile material in excess to nuclear weapons needs and has made much of it available for IAEA safeguards during storage and disposition. In stark contrast to the PRC, the United States makes all our civilian nuclear facilities available for IAEA safeguards and reports annually to the IAEA our civil plutonium holdings. 2000 Steps Nine and Twelve; 2010 Actions Nineteen through Twenty-One: Other Steps for NWS, Transparency, and Reporting: On Steps Nine and Twelve, the P3 commitment to transparency is striking in comparison to the opacity shown by the PRC and Russia. The Working Paper submitted to the 2022 RevCon by the United States, France, and the UK on “Principles and responsible practices for Nuclear Weapon States” represents our shared commitment to transparency and to acting responsibly to reduce nuclear risks, prevent unintended miscalculation and escalation, and support progress on our disarmament commitments. This commitment is also reflected in our NPT national report, which lays out in greater detail the actions I am describing, as

entries.position_summary: The United States affirms that the President holds sole authority to employ nuclear weapons, clarifying centralized command-and-control in its nuclear doctrine. This statement contributes to transparency regarding U.S. nuclear decision-making processes.
entries.quote: Let me be clear, the President of the United States has the sole decision- making authority for U.S. nuclear weapon employment.

entries.position_summary: They emphasize transparency by submitting a detailed national report and adding their Nuclear Posture Review as an official PrepCom addendum, making doctrine information publicly available. This signals active engagement with and support for the NPT review process.
entries.quote: At last year’s Review Conference, we both submitted, and presented at a high level side event, a thorough and detailed national report. This year, we added our Nuclear Posture Review as an addendum; it is available as an official PrepCom document.

entries.position_summary: The country supports improving the NPT review process by adopting Working Group proposals and formalizing interactive discussions of national reports to enhance transparency and accountability. It notes consensus fell just short previously but sees this review cycle as an opportunity to advance these reforms.
entries.quote: In this review cycle, we have a unique opportunity to actually make the review process work better for all of us. The Working Group that met last year identified several concrete proposals that enjoyed broad support, but the group fell just short of consensus, first on a package of recommendations, and in the end on formalizing interactive discussion of national reports as a new mechanism for transparency and accountability.

entries.position_summary: The country rejects claims that U.S. extended deterrence allies participate in U.S. nuclear employment decision-making or violate the NPT, calling such narratives false. It argues these mischaracterizations undermine transparency and dialogue.
entries.quote: We are extremely concerned by false narratives about extended deterrence arrangements and their corrosive effects on transparency and dialogue. Just yesterday, we heard a claim that U.S extended deterrence allies have a role in decision-making with respect to U.S. nuclear weapons employment, and therefore are in violation of their NPT obligations. This is false. The United

entries.position_summary: The state frames its approach around the 2000 Thirteen Steps and the 2010 Action Plan, prioritizing bans/moratoria on nuclear testing, halting and disposing of fissile material production, and ensuring transparency and verification as core disarmament elements. It signals support for CTBT and FMCT-related measures and broader Article VI implementation.
entries.quote: I will frame my abridged remarks around the Thirteen Steps agreed to at the 2000 NPT Review Conference, which addressed nuclear testing, fissile material production and disposition, irreversibility, transparency, and verification, among other priorities, as key elements of disarmament. Our efforts also line up well with the relevant actions from the 2010 Action Plan. Our full statement will be available online.

entries.position_summary: The United States claims it has taken measures to enhance transparency and accountability regarding its nuclear posture. It emphasizes these steps were mostly undertaken outside the formal NPT review process but are accessible to all States Parties.
entries.quote: Let me say a bit more about the steps the United States has already taken in pursuit of transparency and accountability, almost all of it done outside the formal NPT review process but available to States Parties nonetheless.

entries.position_summary: The country emphasizes transparency by inviting ambassadors and NGO experts to visit current and former nuclear weapons facilities, aiming to increase openness and build confidence. These visits serve as confidence-building measures to reduce mistrust regarding its nuclear activities.
entries.quote: That is also why, a few weeks ago, we hosted ambassadors from around the world and nongovernmental experts for visits to current and former nuclear weapons facilities.

entries.position_summary: The speaker contends that, unlike the United States, another state has failed to report its civil plutonium holdings since 2017 and is expanding fissile material production with advanced reactors outside IAEA safeguards. This underscores the speaker’s call for greater transparency in fissile material reporting and for applying IAEA safeguards to relevant facilities.
entries.quote: Nor is it the United States that has declined to report on its civil plutonium holdings since 2017 and is building out its capacity to produce fissile material using advanced nuclear reactors that remain outside of IAEA safeguards.

entries.position_summary: The country urges the PRC to engage in substantive strategic nuclear dialogue to prevent miscalculation and miscommunication. It signals support for risk-reduction, transparency, and potential arms control engagement with China.
entries.quote: It is time for the PRC substantively to engage with us on strategic nuclear issues in order to avoid risks of miscalculation and miscommunication.
